title: Crabmeat

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Crabmeat as a short first-person, mouse-only, point-and-click survival horror experience about operating a crabbing vessel, collecting quota within 7 days, navigating Antarctic waters, maintaining traps/ship functions, and defending against underwater threats. That is enough to infer a horror adventure with light survival/vehicle-management elements, but it does not cleanly map to any single allowed archetype. The current computed hidden_object_puzzle candidate appears overfit to point-and-click/puzzle signals and is not well supported by the actual described loop, while survival_horror/psychological_horror are also not securely established because the evidence does not clearly describe the genre-defining combat/resource/scare structure expected for those archetypes. Strong evidence exists for a hybrid first-person point-and-click horror adventure, but not for a confident placement into the available taxonomy archetypes.

overall_note: The set captures Crabmeat's strongest identity signals best when it focuses on compact vessel operation, maritime threat pressure, first-person interface-driven play, and horror-adventure structure. Top picks fit well, but a few candidates drift toward narrative seafaring RPGs or anthology puzzle adventures rather than Crabmeat's specific first-person point-and-click boat-work loop.
anchor_summary: Crabmeat is a short single-player first-person point-and-click horror adventure centered on operating a small vessel, completing a quota-driven crabbing loop, light ship maintenance/navigation, mouse-only interaction, puzzle-like task flow, and intermittent threat defense in hostile waters.

title: Granblue Fantasy

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Granblue Fantasy as a classic JRPG with a deep turn-based combat system, elemental strategy, classes, party-building, character/weapon progression, and a main quest plus character story episodes. The text strongly supports a story-driven, party-command RPG identity over any other candidate. Real-time co-op raids are present, but they read as an additive mode layered onto the core authored campaign JRPG structure rather than replacing it. The fighting/match-based signals in the prior computation appear to be false positives from words like raids, combos, and co-op rather than evidence of a traditional fighter.

overall_note: The rail is directionally correct because both candidates match the anchor's turn-based, party-based JRPG identity better than action or fighter comps would. The main gap is that neither candidate reflects Granblue Fantasy's raid/co-op layer or broader class/build optimization particularly closely, so the set captures the campaign JRPG side more than the full product shape.
anchor_summary: Granblue Fantasy is best understood here as a story-driven JRPG with turn-based party combat, character/class and weapon progression, elemental strategy, and substantial authored content, with co-op raids as an important secondary mode rather than the core identity.
gap_notes: No candidate mirrors the anchor's notable online co-op raid component.
The set leans toward story-driven turn-based JRPGs but is lighter on class-switching and weapon-grid style progression parallels.
Given the limited candidate pool, both are defensible, but neither feels like an essential benchmark specifically for Granblue Fantasy's mixed solo-plus-raid structure.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very solid fit on strategic turn-based party combat, roster-building, ongoing story delivery, and character progression. It is somewhat looser because its structure emphasizes exploration and live-service progression more than Granblue's class/raid-driven JRPG loop.

title: Thysiastery

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a single-player, turn-based, party-commanded dungeon-crawling roguelike RPG with permadeath, procedural generation, recruitable characters, and skill learning. However, the available taxonomy archetypes do not cleanly fit this identity. The evidence does not establish key archetype-defining traits needed for turn_based_tactics or tactical_rpg, such as tactical-overhead presentation, explicit battlefield structure, or clearer tactical-RPG conventions; nor does it support jrpg_story_rpg, party_based_crpg, or card_battler. Because the gameplay is real but sits between supported archetypes and the strongest candidate mapping would be too inferential, classification should be rejected rather than forced.

overall_note: The rail has a solid center of gravity around turn-based roguelike dungeon RPGs, especially party-focused ones. Best fits emphasize procedural dungeon runs, attrition, permadeath, and party/roster management. The weaker end drifts toward adjacent first-person or single-character roguelikes that share dungeon-crawling and turn-based structure but miss the party-commanded expedition identity.

title: Age of Wonders 4: Rise from Ruin

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted using DLC inheritance from the parent game because the DLC description is additive only: it adds a new culture, form, tomes, wildlife, terrain, quests, and realm traits, and does not describe a different core mode or genre. Parent-game evidence explicitly describes 'Age of Wonders’ signature blend of 4X strategy and turn-based tactical combat,' empire expansion over turns, realm exploration, customizable factions, city/empire growth, tactical battles, and replayable realm generation. The DLC copy reinforces the same identity with battlefield mobility, territory seizure, harvesting ancient wonders, new world-map content, epic quests, and new tomes. This strongly supports 4x_strategy as primary, with turn-based tactical battles as a secondary gameplay layer.

overall_note: The set contains the essential base game and several highly relevant neighboring fantasy 4X strategy titles with tactical combat. Ordering the top tier is straightforward, with only the lower-middle distinctions depending on how much weight to give full 4X structure versus campaign-led tactical strategy.

title: Fly for Fly

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is limited to a single short Metacritic-style description describing a simple comedic action game where the player controls a fly, dodges swats, and annoys students in a classroom. While this suggests arcade-style evasive action with timing-heavy movement, the evidence does not strongly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It does not clearly establish platforming, shooter structure, racing, horror, RPG systems, puzzle structure, tactics, sim management, or another supported core gameplay identity. Because the gameplay appears to fall outside the available archetype set and the source base is sparse, rejection is more reliable than forcing an imprecise fit.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's mischievous chaos premise reasonably well, but most candidates lean more toward sandbox prank games or broad comedy sims than the anchor's tighter timing-and-dodging arcade loop. Best fits emphasize causing irritation through direct control and short-form chaos rather than exploration or destruction for its own sake.
anchor_summary: Fly for Fly appears to be a small-scale comedic arcade action game about controlling a tiny pest, using movement and timing to annoy humans, avoid swats, and survive in a contained environment. Its clearest identity is evasive mischief with simple controls and immediate fail states, not open-ended sandboxing or broad sim progression.

title: Packing Life

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence strongly supports a cozy single-player non-combat puzzle/simulation game centered on organizing, rotating, and fitting items into boxes under space constraints, with light story and workspace customization. However, that gameplay identity does not cleanly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The computed near-match of hidden_object_puzzle is not well supported by the text, which describes spatial packing/optimization rather than hidden object search, point-and-click investigation, or mystery solving. Because the available evidence is strong but indicates an out-of-taxonomy organization/packing puzzle rather than an existing archetype, rejection is more appropriate than forcing an inaccurate fit.

overall_note: The rail has a very strong top end and stays focused on organization-oriented puzzle play. Unpacking is the clearest same-audience match, while A Little to the Left and Wilmot's Warehouse are also credible neighbors from different angles. The weaker end drifts toward broader object-manipulation or inventory-theme comparisons rather than the anchor's specific packing-box optimization loop.
anchor_summary: Packing Life is a cozy single-player non-combat spatial organization puzzle/sim about rotating and fitting varied items into constrained boxes, with light progression, story framing, and a relaxing optimization loop.
gap_notes: The set lacks more direct box-packing or shipment-fulfillment puzzlers, so several picks rely on broader 'tidying/organizing' similarity.
Some candidates share cozy object interaction but not the anchor's exact constrained packing structure and order-completion loop.
The rail is strongest when it emphasizes spatial fitting and organization, weaker when it leans on narrative coziness or object theming alone.

title: Peregrino

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Peregrino as an isometric survival management game centered on crossing a cursed land, scavenging, hunting, fighting, managing scarce resources, inventory pressure, nightfall danger, and extracting with loot. The horror identity is strong via grotesque abominations, cursed woods, and survival-focused threat escalation after dusk. The game also has clear party/caravan management elements: caring for companions, leading pilgrims, making fate-affecting decisions, and upgrading caravan crafting stations. That makes it broader than pure survival horror, so survival_horror fits best as the primary archetype with tactical_rpg and party_based_crpg as secondary influences rather than full identities.

overall_note: The rail has a solid center of gravity around party or caravan survival under attrition, with several candidates matching Peregrino's travel-management loop well. Best fits emphasize expedition structure, supplies, companion pressure, and difficult decisions; weaker fits drift toward formal turn-based tactics or roguelike combat loops over survival management.
anchor_summary: Peregrino is an isometric single-player survival management journey built around leading a caravan/party through a hostile horror landscape, balancing scavenging, resource scarcity, camp or caravan upkeep, companion care, route risk, and tactical survival decisions rather than pure action-horror.
gap_notes: The strongest overlaps are caravan or group survival-management games; the set is less consistent on Peregrino's day exploration/night refuge cadence.
Several candidates match tone and journey structure better than they match the anchor's scavenging, extraction, and inventory-pressure loop.
The rail leans somewhat too heavily on turn-based tactical RPGs, which captures party pressure but can miss the anchor's survival-management identity.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on caravan leadership, harsh travel, supply management, crew welfare, route planning, and survival in a hostile world, though it is more narrative CRPG/trading oriented than horror-survival.

Excellent match on scarcity, shelter upkeep, survivor needs, day/night pressure, scavenging runs, and hard moral survival choices, though it lacks the anchor's caravan-trek structure and broader party expedition framing.
Strong caravan-journey and follower-management overlap with consequential survival decisions, but its identity is more narrative tactical RPG than systemic scavenging and survival management.

Good fit on leading a roaming party through a hostile world with camp upkeep, resources, crafting, and attrition, but the core loop is more open-world mercenary tactics than horror survival management.

Shares bleak road-trip structure, party stress, coach upgrades, and horror-driven attrition, but its run-based roguelike and combat-forward design is a looser fit than games centered on survival logistics and caravan management.

title: Pieced Together

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a short, single-player scrapbook puzzler built around sorting mementos, reassembling torn photos, and solving gentle logic/word puzzles across handcrafted pages while uncovering an authored story about friendship and aging. There is no combat, the tone is explicitly cozy and emotional, and interaction appears cursor-driven through scrapbook pages. While the copy does not strongly emphasize classic hidden-object searching, the closest supported taxonomy fit is hidden_object_puzzle because the core loop is light object-focused puzzle interaction in handcrafted scenes/pages with narrative progression.

overall_note: The rail has a solid grasp of the anchor's cozy, object-centered, narrative-puzzle identity. The top three are strong fits, but the back half drifts toward narrative-adventure and interactive-fiction comps that share mood and memoir themes more than the same core loop.
anchor_summary: Pieced Together is a very short, single-player cozy narrative puzzler built around sorting keepsakes, reassembling photos, solving gentle logic/word tasks, and decorating scrapbook pages to uncover an intimate authored story. Best matches should share low-pressure object manipulation, light puzzle gating, handcrafted progression, and story delivery through personal items rather than action or broad exploration.
gap_notes: Top candidates appropriately focus on tactile object interaction, sorting, arrangement, and gentle authored progression.
The set leans a bit too heavily on emotional/artistic tone in lower ranks; similarity should stay anchored in light object-puzzle play rather than memoir presentation alone.
No candidate is a base-game pairing or near-identical gameplay match, so the rail is good but not excellent.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit: cozy single-player object manipulation, environmental organization, gentle puzzle structure, decoration, and intimate story revealed through personal belongings.
Excellent match on casual tactile sorting/arrangement puzzles and low-pressure play, though it is less narrative-led and less memoir-focused than the anchor.
Short, cozy, story-driven puzzle game centered on handling meaningful objects; the repair/disassembly focus differs, but the emotional authored progression is very compatible.

title: Mirage 7

That combination supports a cinematic action-adventure identity more strongly than RPG or open-world labels. World topology is not stated explicitly, but the copy emphasizes a guided tale and handcrafted challenges rather than sandbox or systemic play.

overall_note: The set captures Mirage 7's general third-person story-adventure identity reasonably well, especially on exploration, puzzles, and accessible action. Best fits are authored action-adventures with environmental traversal and light progression; weaker fits drift toward pure puzzle adventure, stealth-driven structure, or open-world action RPG.

title: Fortuna Magus

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DB evidence consistently describes Fortuna Magus as a fantasy JRPG with a rich authored story, single-player campaign structure, and strategic turn-based party battles. Core gameplay details are concrete and repeated across Steam short/detailed descriptions and Metacritic: characters pursue a missing-father narrative, battles revolve around Revelations, TP management, tandem attacks, and learning new spells/skills through combat conditions and element thresholds. That supports a story-led JRPG identity with party management, magic-focused turn-based combat, quest-driven progression, and skill customization. Alternative candidates like turn_based_tactics are weaker because there is no evidence of grid tactics or overhead tactical structure, while monster_collect_rpg lacks creature-collection evidence.

overall_note: The set stays mostly on-target for story-driven turn-based JRPGs. The best fits are other compact, linear party-based JRPGs with strong progression systems, especially Kemco-style titles. A few candidates are solid but broader retro-JRPG neighbors rather than especially tight gameplay matches.
anchor_summary: Fortuna Magus is a single-player, story-led turn-based JRPG built around party management, authored progression, skill customization, and battle-specific resource/condition systems like TP, tandem attacks, elemental growth, and unlockable abilities.
gap_notes: The rail would be stronger with more direct Kemco/EXE-CREATE-style turn-based JRPG comps centered on compact campaign structure and layered skill unlock systems.

title: Messy Up

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence establishes a cute multiplayer pet-chaos game with co-op, PvP, and asymmetric roles (pets cause destruction, owner catches/defends), plus collectibles and a home-building mode. However, the material does not clearly anchor the game to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The strongest signals are party/co-op/social chaos, but the evidence does not concretely define a match-based party-game structure, nor does it support action-adventure, platformer, shooter, racer, sim, or RPG archetypes in the allowed list. The current taxonomy audit already flagged a provider gap with no candidate archetypes. Because the gameplay description is broad, mode-mixed, and not strongly aligned to a supported archetype, classification should be rejected for now.

overall_note: The candidate set captures the anchor's strongest gameplay identity: animal-led chaos, household/environment interaction, accessible multiplayer, and slapstick disruption. Best fits are games built around mischievous animals and sandbox havoc; weaker fits drift toward pure brawler-party or task-coordination structures rather than asymmetric pet-destruction play.

title: Banquet for Fools

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Banquet for Fools as a single-player, open-world, party-based RPG centered on investigating a village disappearance across the island of Invimona. Core loop evidence is strong: exploring towns and holds for clues, taking quests/requests, managing four companions with stat-driven dialogue options and bonding, and progressing characters through skills, spells, songs, equipment, and level-ups. Combat is explicitly described as brawl-style beat-'em-up action with pausable party command decisions, leader switching, support combos, and coordinated attacks, supporting a western_narrative_rpg classification with beat_em_up as a secondary flavor rather than the primary identity. The mystery-driven quest structure, party management, authored campaign framing, and open-world exploration are all directly evidenced in the Steam detailed description and echoed by the short description/Metacritic copy.

overall_note: The rail has a solid center around party-based real-time RPGs with exploration and companion management, but several picks drift toward either much more classic CRPG structure or looser single-character action RPGs. The best fits are games combining party control, open-area exploration, and pause-capable or hybrid real-time combat.
anchor_summary: Banquet for Fools appears to be a single-player open-world party action RPG with a mystery-solving quest spine, four-character party management, exploratory island travel, and brawl-style real-time combat that includes pausing for command selection, leader switching, and combo coordination. Its identity leans more toward western party-based narrative action RPG than JRPG, with investigation and navigation challenge as notable differentiators.
gap_notes: No candidate closely matches the anchor's specific brawl/beat-'em-up combat feel combined with four-character party control.
The mystery-investigation and clue-driven exploration loop is only partially represented across the set.
Some candidates match party exploration well but differ substantially in combat structure or overall RPG tradition.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Large-scale party RPG with real-time combat plus tactical pause, companion relationships, open-area quest exploration, and strong party-management overlap despite being more cinematic and system-heavy.

title: Cupiclaw

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Cupiclaw as a 'cute & frantic claw machine roguelike deckbuilder' centered on grabbing prizes, building combos, customizing runs, upgrading the claw, and facing increasingly chaotic machines. That is enough to establish a clear gameplay identity, but the identity does not cleanly map to any allowed archetype in the taxonomy. It is not supported as sports_sim, realistic_racer, or pinball despite the current computed near-misses, and it also does not have sufficient evidence of card-battle combat to justify card_battler. Because the core loop appears to be an arcade claw-machine roguelike/deckbuilding hybrid outside the available archetype set, rejection is more appropriate than forcing a misleading fit.

overall_note: The rail has a solid grasp of Cupiclaw's unusual hybrid identity: arcade-device interaction plus roguelike combo-building. Best candidates emphasize physics/board-driven runs and emergent synergies. The main weakness is that some picks drift toward combat-centric roguelites rather than prize-machine or score-engine style play.
anchor_summary: Cupiclaw is a single-player arcade/strategy roguelike deckbuilder built around claw-machine interactions: grabbing prizes, assembling combo synergies, upgrading the claw, and surviving increasingly chaotic run-based machines. The closest fits should share run-based drafting/buildcraft and a tactile physics/arcade board layer, not just generic roguelite structure.
gap_notes: Best fits are games where the central action is operating or aiming within a machine/board system while drafting synergies across runs.
Combat-heavy roguelites with enemies and class abilities are somewhat less faithful than score/combo engine builders built around arcade randomness.
No candidate is a near-identical match for the claw-machine-specific interaction, so even the top pick is a strong neighbor rather than 'same'.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on run-based drafting, arcade-machine feel, kinetic board interactions, and absurd combo engines. Pachinko scoring differs from claw grabbing, but the physics-plus-synergy identity is highly compatible.

Excellent match on roguelike deckbuilder structure, randomized drafting, and payoff-combo construction inside a gambling-machine frame. Less tactile/physics-driven than Cupiclaw, but very aligned in buildcraft and run logic.

Shares physics-driven aiming, run progression, relic/orb synergies, and arcade roots. However, Peglin is fundamentally turn-based enemy combat, which is a meaningful step away from Cupiclaw's machine-management combo loop.

The pinball physics and roguelite runs are relevant, but Roundguard is primarily a dungeon crawler with character classes, loot, and combat encounters. That makes it less faithful to Cupiclaw's prize-machine/deckbuilder identity.

title: Bulb Boy 2: Jar of Despair

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store/metadata evidence consistently describes Bulb Boy 2 as a single-player point-and-click horror adventure built around solving tricky puzzles, navigating eerie handcrafted spaces, and unfolding a linear twisted story. Core signals are strong for cursor-driven interaction, environmental puzzle solving, authored linear progression, mystery/survival-escape framing, and essentially non-combat-first play. The text does mention facing jelly monsters and a boss, so I treated combat as light rather than none, but the dominant gameplay identity still aligns best with hidden_object_puzzle / point-and-click puzzle adventure. Horror tone is strong but not enough to override the puzzle-first structure as the primary archetype.

overall_note: The rail has a strong grasp of the anchor’s puzzle-first horror-adventure identity. It correctly includes the original Bulb Boy and several close horror point-and-click neighbors, but the lower end drifts toward games with weaker puzzle density or a different structure/tone emphasis.
anchor_summary: Bulb Boy 2: Jar of Despair is a single-player 2D point-and-click horror adventure centered on cursor-driven environmental puzzles, light/no real combat emphasis, grotesque cartoon imagery, and authored linear story progression.
gap_notes: Bulb Boy should be treated as the essential benchmark/base-series comp and ranked first.
Fran Bow is a very strong fit, but its heavier narrative/psychological framing and distinct mechanics make it slightly less direct than Bulb Boy.
Bad Dream: Coma fits the point-and-click horror puzzle space well, though its more nonlinear action-reaction structure is a bit less aligned with the anchor’s authored linearity.
The Darkside Detective matches interface and puzzle investigation structure, but its comedic episodic case format is a looser fit than the darker, more continuous horror adventures above it.
Little Misfortune is the weakest fit here because it is more interactive narrative/exploration than puzzle-gated point-and-click adventure.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct predecessor in the same series with nearly identical point-and-click horror-adventure structure, grotesque cartoon style, linear progression, and puzzle-led play.

title: Ratcheteer DX

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently describe an interconnected action-adventure with more than 250 rooms across multiple regions and dungeons, eight tools that unlock new paths, and explicit traversal verbs like gliding, dashing, drilling, and stomping. The structure of gated exploration through a connected world plus boss encounters strongly supports metroidvania. The copy also supports a 2D platform-navigation-forward presentation and single-player campaign progression.

overall_note: The rail is directionally solid: all four candidates share exploration-driven action-adventure structure with dungeons, item progression, and boss gates. The main gap is that most picks lean Zelda-like top-down adventure rather than a tighter 2D metroidvania/platform-action identity, so the set is more adjacent than exact.
anchor_summary: Ratcheteer DX appears to be a single-player lo-fi action-adventure/metroidvania: interconnected 2D room-based exploration, item/tool-gated progression, traversal ability unlocks, dungeon and boss milestones, and light-to-moderate combat.
gap_notes: Missing a clearer 2D side-view metroidvania peer; several candidates are top-down Zelda-likes instead of platform-navigation-forward matches.

No obvious bad inclusion, but the set skews broader action-adventure rather than closest gameplay-identity comps.

candidate_reviews.rationale: 2D action-adventure with room-to-room exploration, dungeons, light combat, items unlocking deeper areas, and a nonlinear connected world; closest structural fit despite a more Zelda-like emphasis.

title: Docked

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Docked as a realistic port-operations simulator centered on operating heavy machinery, completing mission-based jobs and contracts, repairing equipment, expanding infrastructure, purchasing lots and machinery, and growing a logistics business. That supports transport_sim most strongly, with a management layer as secondary rather than primary. The current computed taxonomy over-indexes on racing because of vehicle language, but the actual gameplay loop is commercial cargo handling and operational expansion, not racing competition.

overall_note: The proposed rail has a solid center around heavy-equipment and work-sim games, but it mixes in a DLC listing and a few looser vehicle/business sims that drift away from Docked’s port-operations identity.
anchor_summary: Docked is a realistic, mission-driven heavy-operations simulator about operating specialized machinery, fulfilling contracts, repairing equipment, expanding infrastructure, and growing a port business. Its closest comps should emphasize hands-on vehicle/machinery tasks plus operational progression rather than racing or pure management.
gap_notes: Construction-heavy and vehicle-work simulators fit best because Docked combines precise machinery operation with contract progression and company growth.
SnowRunner and Euro Truck Simulator 2 share logistics and vehicle handling, but they are less about site operations, equipment maintenance, and rebuilding a fixed business hub.
Farming Simulator 22 matches the machinery/business loop broadly, but its agricultural sandbox structure is a looser fit than construction or recovery sims.
Euro Truck Simulator 2: Iberia is specifically a DLC/map expansion rather than the base game, which weakens it as a clean similar-games recommendation for the anchor.

title: The Walking Trade

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present the game as a shop/store management sandbox first: designing the shop layout, organizing shelves, setting prices, managing staff, crafting stock and upgrades, and building barricades/traps. That strongly fits management_tycoon with systemic sandbox/base growth. The zombie defense layer is significant but appears additive to the management loop rather than the main identity; combat is described as defending the shop during raids and hordes, not as the core session structure. Colony_sim and city_builder are nearby matches because of survivor assignment, construction, and base defense, but the clearest central fantasy is operating and optimizing a business in an apocalypse.

overall_note: Both candidates connect through survival management and base growth, but neither cleanly matches the anchor's business/store-running core loop. Survivalist: Invisible Strain is the stronger gameplay neighbor because of survivor management, base building, and zombie pressure; Mr. Prepper is a looser fit centered more on solo survival crafting than commerce or staffed shop operations.
anchor_summary: The Walking Trade is primarily a single-player shop-management/survival sandbox: design and optimize a store, manage inventory/pricing/staff, craft upgrades, build defenses, and directly repel zombie raids. Its identity is management-tycoon first, with colony/base-defense and light survival-action layers.
gap_notes: The candidate set lacks a true shopkeeper/merchant-management comp, which is the anchor's clearest differentiator.
Most similarity here comes from survival, crafting, scavenging, and base development rather than pricing, retail layout, and customer-service loops.
Combat/base defense overlap is present, but the rail underserves the anchor's hybrid of tycoon management plus active zombie defense.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Strong overlap in recruiting/equipping survivors, base development, scavenging, and zombie-driven defense decisions. Weaker on the anchor's store-management, pricing, and retail optimization loop, so it is adjacent rather than truly same.

title: Sushi Cat - Tower Defense

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text consistently identifies the game as a roguelite tower defense: placing towers, walls, and traps, shaping enemy paths, surviving wave-based encounters, drafting Boon Cards, fighting boss enemies each run, and unlocking permanent upgrades between runs. That is strong evidence for the game's gameplay identity, but the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 does not include a tower defense / tower-defense roguelite archetype. Available archetypes such as rts, turn_based_tactics, card_battler, or co_op_action_roguelite would misrepresent the core play loop. Because the evidence is strong yet does not map cleanly to an allowed archetype, this title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate classification.

overall_note: The candidate is a very close gameplay match because it also centers on roguelite tower defense runs with path-shaping placement decisions, wave defense, randomized run structure, and replayability through unlocks and modifiers.
anchor_summary: Sushi Cat - Tower Defense is a single-player roguelite tower defense game built around short runs, wave defense on a single evolving map, maze-building with walls/traps/towers, boss waves, run-defining boon/card drafting, and meta-progression unlocks between runs.
gap_notes: The anchor emphasizes a single evolving level, boss encounters, and explicit meta-progression/talent upgrades, while the candidate leans more into tile-placement/board-game puzzle structure.
The candidate is slightly more puzzle-forward in its tile-draw and expanding-island format than the anchor's more direct tower/wall/trap maze-building loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Roguelite tower defense with short replayable runs, randomized build pieces, path-building, wave defense, and unlock-driven progression; one of the clearest genre matches available.

title: Never Grave: The Witch and the Curse

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a 'Metroidvania x Roguelite' with side-scrolling 2D action, exploration emphasis, traversal unlocks like double-jump, dungeon obstacles and puzzles, and intense boss fights. The core loop also includes procedurally generated dungeon runs, loot/material collection, and village rebuilding with facilities, crops, cooking, and skill-tree growth, which supports roguelite metaprogression as a secondary identity rather than displacing the metroidvania core. Up to 4-player co-op is explicit, but the strongest gameplay identity remains exploration-driven action-platforming with ability-gated progression.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's hybrid identity reasonably well, especially its metroidvania-plus-roguelite side. Best fits are other 2D action games that combine exploration, procedural or run-based structure, and persistent progression. The main gap is that several candidates miss the anchor's specific mix of co-op, exploration gating, and roguelite metaprogression all at once.
anchor_summary: 2D side-scrolling action-platformer that blends metroidvania exploration and traversal unlocks with roguelite procedural dungeon runs, persistent village/base rebuilding, skill-tree growth, tough boss fights, and up to 4-player co-op.
gap_notes: Only Sundered and Dragon Marked for Death match the anchor's 4-player co-op profile; most candidates are single-player only.
Dead Cells and Rogue Legacy 2 are strong roguelite action-platformer comps, but both are looser on the anchor's co-op and village-rebuild metaprogression identity.
Dragon Marked for Death matches side-scrolling co-op action well, but its quest/stage-based action RPG structure is notably less metroidvania/roguelite than the anchor.
Chasm matches proceduralized metroidvania exploration, but lacks the anchor's co-op and stronger roguelite run-loop emphasis.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on 2D metroidvania combat-platforming, procedural/world-shifting structure, traversal unlocks, bosses, and even up-to-4-player co-op; less run-based and less base-building than the anchor.

title: Pokémon Pokopia

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is limited to a single promotional description emphasizing a cozy rebuilding premise: playing as Ditto, crafting, learning Pokémon moves, and restoring a desolate world. While this suggests buildcraft and systemic world-shaping, it does not concretely establish the core gameplay loop, perspective, session structure, management depth, or whether the title plays primarily as life sim, farming sim, city/colony builder, or another simulation form. With only sparse, high-level copy and no stronger gameplay-specific evidence, classification would be too speculative.

overall_note: The proposed rail is directionally solid because most candidates emphasize cozy rebuilding, crafting, settlement growth, and exploration. The strongest fits are games centered on restoring ruined spaces through gathering and building. A few picks drift toward broader life-sim or creature-ranching neighbors rather than the same gameplay identity.
anchor_summary: Pokémon Pokopia appears to be a cozy third-person rebuilding/crafting sandbox or life-sim adventure: gather help from Pokémon, use Ditto transformation abilities and learned moves to reshape terrain, craft, travel, and gradually restore a ruined world into a livable community. The clearest identity signals are world restoration, buildcraft, and systemic environment interaction rather than traditional Pokémon battling or RPG structure.
gap_notes: The rail leans heavily on cozy restoration fantasy, but the anchor also suggests terrain-changing/systemic world-shaping mechanics that are more specifically matched by builder-sandbox games.
There is some uncertainty around the anchor's exact combat and management depth, so life-sim comparisons are acceptable but should rank below hands-on crafting/building restorers.

title: Oceanhorn 3: Legend of the Shadow Sea

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence establishes only a broad open-world action/adventure premise: sailing across islands, battling giant Titans, and pursuing a long-lost god. However, the DB evidence is too sparse to confidently place it into a specific V2 archetype. Core identity axes needed for acceptance remain ungrounded, especially perspective, concrete combat style/tempo, progression model, and whether the RPG framing is substantial enough for open_world_fantasy_action_rpg versus broader open_world_action_adventure. With only a short Metacritic description and one normalized genre label, the classification would be too inferential.

overall_note: The rail has solid thematic and gameplay-adjacent picks, with the two prior Oceanhorn games as the most natural inclusions. The broader open-world comps also make sense, though they are looser fits because they emphasize larger systemic open-world play rather than the series' more curated island-adventure structure.
anchor_summary: Oceanhorn 3: Legend of the Shadow Sea appears to be a single-player fantasy action-adventure/action-RPG centered on sailing between islands, open-world or open-zone exploration, real-time combat against large creatures, puzzle/quest progression, and traversal features like gliding. Evidence is sparse, but the clearest identity is Zelda-like exploratory action-adventure rather than a pure story RPG.
gap_notes: The best fits are the franchise neighbors; they should anchor the rail.

No evidence points more strongly to soulslike, shooter, horror, or RPG-first structures.

title: Rotwood

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Rotwood as a 1-4 player hack-and-slash roguelike/dungeon crawler built around repeat runs, co-op combat, boss fights, weapon mastery, gear upgrading, and bringing spoils back to craft equipment and improve a safe haven. The text explicitly says 'every run offers different powers and abilities,' calls it a 'rogue-like dungeon crawler,' emphasizes combat mastery over randomness, and highlights increasingly difficult encounters and powerful corrupted bosses. Metacritic also labels it a '2D Beat-'Em-Up,' which supports the side-scrolling/beat-em-up secondary read, but the stronger identity is cooperative action roguelite due to run-based structure, repeatable progression, and meta/base advancement.

overall_note: The candidate set captures Rotwood’s co-op action-roguelite side well at the top, especially with games centered on repeat runs, melee combat, and boss progression. The weaker half drifts toward broader co-op action or classic dungeon crawling that misses Rotwood’s specific mix of run-based structure, combat mastery, and brawler-like feel.

title: NINJA GAIDEN 4 The Two Masters

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DLC descriptions consistently describe a combat-centric action game built around three new campaign chapters, unique levels, new weapons, bosses, fiend enemies, deadly trials, and an endurance-style challenge mode with successive combat encounters. The weapon copy emphasizes ranged attack, crowd control, rapid strikes, closing distance, and testing skilled 'master ninja' play, which strongly supports a fast, mastery-heavy character-action identity. The campaign/chapter structure and boss/trial language support linear level-based progression rather than open-world or RPG-first play. Horror imagery is present in the setting and story framing, but the gameplay evidence is much more strongly centered on stylish high-intensity combat than on horror as the primary play identity.

overall_note: The rail has the essential inclusion in the base game and two credible character-action neighbors. It is directionally correct, but beyond the base game the set leans more toward broader stylish-action comps than near-identical Ninja Gaiden-style fits.
anchor_summary: NINJA GAIDEN 4 The Two Masters is a direct DLC extension of a fast, mission/chapter-based character-action game: high-speed melee combat, weapon-specific movesets, boss encounters, trials, and an endurance challenge mode built around precision and mastery.
gap_notes: The base game is an essential inclusion and clearly the best match.

title: Apopia: Sugar Coated Tale

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a single-player hand-drawn narrative adventure with puzzles, rhythm segments, minigames, light action, and character-focused mystery. However, the copy does not establish a stable core gameplay loop, perspective, traversal model, or dominant mechanics strongly enough to support any allowed V2 archetype. It is not clearly a visual novel, hidden-object puzzle game, action-platformer, psychological horror title, or cinematic action-adventure based on the available text. The gameplay appears intentionally mixed and provider copy stays broad, so the evidence is insufficient for a confident archetype assignment.

overall_note: The rail mostly understands the anchor as a story-driven indie adventure with whimsical presentation, emotional mystery, and mechanic variety. Best fits are games that combine character-focused exploration with puzzles or music/minigame interactions. A few candidates lean too far toward RPG combat systems or near-visual-novel interactive story structures versus Apopia’s broader adventure-plus-minigame profile.

title: Scott Pilgrim EX

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Classification is strongly supported by the stored store/editorial copy plus a direct Metacritic genre label of '2D Beat-'Em-Up.' The text repeatedly frames the game as a 'brand-new brawling adventure' centered on fighting through Toronto, stringing together stylish combos, using weapons and special attacks, battling enemies and bosses, and supporting up to four-player co-op. It also specifies a 2D beat-'em-up identity, seven fighters with distinct combat styles, and a city of interconnected levels with quests and hidden areas, which fits a campaign-driven beat_em_up with semi-open progression and light action-adventure structure rather than a pure linear stage arcade brawler. Progression is reinforced by badges, stat upgrades, coins, and customizable builds.

overall_note: The rail is solid and correctly focused on modern beat-'em-ups. Its strongest hit is River City Girls 2 because it mirrors not just the brawling but also the connected-world, quest, shop, and progression structure. The rest are valid genre neighbors, though TMNT and Streets of Rage 4 skew more arcade-linear than the anchor, and Castle Crashers is a looser but still understandable co-op brawler comp.
anchor_summary: Scott Pilgrim EX is a modern 2D beat-'em-up centered on combo-driven melee combat, multiple distinct fighters, light build/stat progression, quests, interconnected city exploration, and up-to-4-player drop-in co-op. Best matches should preserve the side-scrolling brawler core loop first, with extra credit for campaign progression, explorable structure, and party co-op.
gap_notes: The set lacks the obvious Scott Pilgrim benchmark: Scott Pilgrim vs. The World: The Game would be an expected must-include if available.
The current list leans heavily on arcade-stage beat-'em-ups; the anchor's interconnected-city exploration and quest structure are only strongly reflected by River City Girls 2.
A stronger rail would balance pure genre comps with more progression-forward, semi-open campaign brawlers.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit: side-scrolling co-op beat-'em-up combat plus leveling, shops, items, quests, multiple fighters, and a connected city to explore rather than only linear stages.

title: Esoteric Ebb

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Esoteric Ebb as an isometric single-player CRPG with deep branching dialogue, abundant choices, open-ended quests, dice-driven TTRPG-style encounters, investigation of a political conspiracy, and exploration of a fantasy city. The strongest identity signals are authored branching narrative, quest-solving, detective-mystery framing, and classic CRPG structure rather than party tactics or action combat. Combat is explicitly de-emphasized ('violence as a last resort'), so western_narrative_rpg fits better than party_based_crpg or combat-forward RPG archetypes.

overall_note: This candidate set is very strong and captures the anchor's dialogue-first, investigation-led CRPG identity well. Disco Elysium is an obvious essential benchmark, with the Torment games as close philosophical/text-heavy CRPG neighbors. Citizen Sleeper and Pentiment are looser but still credible because they share choice-driven investigation and low-combat narrative progression.
anchor_summary: Esoteric Ebb is a single-player isometric narrative CRPG with strong Disco Elysium DNA: investigation-heavy questing, branching dialogue, internal stat/voice interplay, dice-driven checks, failure-forward roleplay, and combat treated as a secondary or last-resort solution.
gap_notes: The rail is strongest on dialogue-first narrative RPGs, but it is somewhat light on other modern isometric CRPGs with explicit dice-check/skill-check quest structure.

title: iRacing Arcade

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store copy is strong and concrete: the game is repeatedly described as a 'third-person arcade racer' focused on licensed car racing, online races, global time trials, and weekly events. Core play is competing in races, chasing lap times, and progressing through a motorsport legacy structure by earning resources and investing in team growth, but the identity remains vehicular racing first. The 'easy to learn yet endlessly rewarding' physics, stylized recreations of licensed cars/tracks, and explicit arcade-racer phrasing support arcade_racer over realistic_racer, while the management layer appears secondary rather than transforming it into a sim/tycoon game.

title: The Abbess Garden

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator descriptions consistently describe a single-player, third-person cozy gardening game set in historical France. The core loop is restoring and tending a garden in a free-form natural space, growing and identifying plants through simulated ecosystem factors like soil, moisture, and plant proximity, while progressing gentle quests, uncovering hidden objects, helping NPCs, and pursuing romance. The game is explicitly peaceful and non-combat, with linear story advancement layered onto an at-your-own-pace life/gardening routine. That aligns best with farming_sim, with life_sim as a secondary influence due to social/romance and community-help elements.

overall_note: The rail has a solid center around plant care, restoration, and cozy progression, but it mixes true gardening/restoration comps with puzzle-forward or broader farming-life sims that drift away from the anchor’s specific free-form garden-tending identity.

title: World of Warcraft: Midnight

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored text focuses almost entirely on setting, zones, and story context for Azeroth/Quel'Thalas/Xal'atath, without concrete gameplay loop, combat, progression, perspective, or mode details. title: Soulshard

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Soulshard as a 2D side-scrolling platformer built around 30 discrete levels, trap avoidance, and using the environment to progress, with single-player and local co-op play. The copy does not establish meaningful combat, RPG systems, exploration structure, or action-platformer fighting, which points away from action_platformer and toward a non-combat precision/platform-navigation identity. Minimalist pixel-art presentation and a dark limbo-like setting are also directly supported by the store text.

overall_note: The candidate set is directionally solid: most picks understand the anchor as a co-op-capable, level-based 2D platformer centered on traversal and hazards rather than combat. The best matches are co-op puzzle/precision platformers with stage-based progression. The main weakness is that some candidates lean more heavily into bespoke puzzle-gimmicks or combat than the anchor evidence supports.
anchor_summary: Soulshard appears to be a non-combat 2D precision/puzzle-platformer built around discrete trap-heavy levels, environmental interaction, hazard avoidance, and optional local co-op, with a casual-to-moderate platforming profile rather than combat or exploration-driven progression.
gap_notes: No candidate looks like an exact same-identity match for a minimalist non-combat trap-avoidance platformer with optional local co-op.

title: Aquamarine: Explorer's Edition

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently portray Aquamarine as a single-player, turn-based sci-fi survival/exploration adventure focused on foraging, cultivation, salvage, loadout upgrades, navigational challenges, and environmental puzzle solving in an alien ocean. The strongest supported signals are cursor-driven/point-and-click interaction, authored linear mystery framing, non-combat play, and environmental puzzles. However, none of the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes cleanly fits this combination. It is not well-supported as hidden_object_puzzle because the evidence emphasizes survival, navigation, and environmental problem solving rather than hidden-object scenes. It is not well-supported as management_tycoon because there is no strong business/optimization/tycoon loop. It also does not cleanly map to exploration_survival_adventure because the available archetype implies a more standard real-time survival-adventure identity than the game's turn-based, comic-book, puzzle-forward structure. Because the evidence is solid about what the game is, but does not strongly support any allowed archetype, rejection is the safest taxonomy outcome.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's survival-exploration side reasonably well, especially around expedition pressure, resource management, and discovery. The main gap is that Aquamarine is notably turn-based, puzzle-forward, and largely non-combat, while several candidates skew more toward action, party management, or narrative sailing systems than environmental problem solving.
anchor_summary: Aquamarine: Explorer's Edition is a single-player, turn-based survival/exploration adventure with strong environmental puzzle solving, resource upkeep, scavenging/cultivation/crafting, and mystery-driven progression in an alien ocean. Its identity is closer to a slow, non-combat, expedition-survival puzzle game than to a tycoon or hidden-object game.
gap_notes: No candidate closely matches Aquamarine's specific blend of turn-based survival, environmental puzzle solving, and low/no-combat progression.
Several picks match exploration and survival pressure but miss the anchor's puzzle-forward, authored-mystery structure.
The set leans toward broader survival/exploration comps rather than games with similar interaction style and challenge model.

The corpus also explicitly says players can freely switch between first- and third-person views, supporting a modern single-player campaign survival-horror structure rather than a purely cinematic or purely psychological horror title. Story setup emphasizes investigating mysterious deaths in Raccoon City while escaping lethal threats, which supports detective_mystery plus survival_escape within an authored linear campaign.

title: Laysara: Summit Kingdom

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Laysara: Summit Kingdom as a 'challenging city builder' and a 'pure city building experience' focused on economy, resource management, production chains, settlement expansion, transport/logistics optimization, hazard mitigation, and rebuilding across multiple mountain towns. The core loop is establishing and expanding settlements, satisfying population needs, building trade and transport networks, and solving environmental constraints like avalanches and weather. The text also directly states there is no combat or military aspect. This strongly supports city_builder as the primary archetype, with management_tycoon/colony_sim as adjacent secondary readings due to resource chains, needs management, and multi-settlement administration.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's city-building, logistics, and environment-pressure identity fairly well, but it leans heavily toward survival-flavored builders and misses a clearer pure-economy/logistics peer. The best fits are games that combine settlement planning, supply chains, terrain or hazard management, and multi-settlement or network thinking without shifting too far into combat or narrative survival drama.
anchor_summary: Laysara: Summit Kingdom is a combat-free, single-player city builder centered on settlement growth, production chains, transport-network optimization, population-needs management, and surviving harsh environmental constraints across multiple interconnected mountain towns.
gap_notes: The set overindexes on survival-city-builder pressure relative to Laysara's more economy-and-logistics-first identity.

title: The New Zealand Story: Untold Adventure

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a colorful 2D platformer and remake of the 1988 platformer The NewZealand Story. The gameplay loop is described as jumping and running through handcrafted levels, uncovering hidden areas, using weapons, vehicles, and power-ups, and facing challenging enemies and bosses. This supports a level-based side-scrolling action-platformer identity with platform navigation as the core verb and combat as a supporting but meaningful layer. Although the computed near-miss/candidate system surfaced beat_em_up from side-scrolling + melee + bosses, the text strongly centers platforming, responsive movement, precision, hidden secrets, and arcade stage challenge rather than crowd-control brawling, making action_platformer the better fit.

overall_note: The rail has the right general neighborhood: classic and revival-era 2D action-platformers with level-based progression and old-school challenge. Best fits are arcade-rooted stage-clear platformers; the weakest entry is the multi-game Wonder Boy compilation because its included games span several gameplay identities rather than matching the anchor cleanly.

Multiple store/editorial descriptions explicitly call it a 'visual novel' and describe '60k of text,' '2-4 hours of reading,' and 'a linear narrative with no choices or branches.' The gameplay identity is therefore narrative-reading focused rather than action, strategy, or exploration driven. Supporting facets include single-player mode, cute anime-style presentation, casual genre labeling, and absence of combat mechanics. The premise centers on character interaction between the demon queen and princess, supporting an authored linear interpersonal drama structure within a fantasy setting.

overall_note: The set is directionally correct because all candidates are yuri story-led visual novels centered on reading and character relationships. title: Afterplace

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently describe Afterplace as a single-player open-world adventure focused on free exploration, hidden paths, secrets, dungeons, monster fighting, NPC conversations, and multiple endings driven by player choices. The strongest identity signals are open-world exploration, handcrafted discovery, quest/exploration progression, branching narrative outcomes, and active melee-oriented combat against monsters. RPG elements like weapon upgrades, armor purchases, and choice-driven endings support a secondary western_narrative_rpg read, but the dominant presentation in the evidence is open-world action-adventure rather than a deeper stat- or party-driven RPG. Perspective is not explicitly stated in the stored evidence, but the remaining evidence is strong enough to support the archetype.

overall_note: The set is directionally strong for top-down exploration-driven action-adventure, with several good discovery-first comps. Best fits emphasize open-ended roaming, secrets, light-to-moderate combat, and handcrafted world discovery; weaker fits skew more linear, more punishing, or more dungeon-gated than Afterplace’s freer wandering structure.
anchor_summary: Afterplace is a single-player top-down/open-world action-adventure with light action-RPG elements: freeform exploration, hidden paths and dungeons, simple hack-and-slash combat, NPC conversations and choices, light gear upgrading, and a low-handholding discovery-driven structure.
gap_notes: The rail trends slightly toward tighter, more challenge-forward action games than Afterplace’s more relaxed exploratory identity.

title: Tales of Berseria Remastered

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and metadata evidence consistently describe a story-driven action RPG centered on Velvet and her companions, indicating a campaign-focused JRPG structure rather than a loot or open-world identity. The description explicitly emphasizes a revenge journey across Midgand, a cast of companions, authored story themes, destination markers, and a combat system built around choosing Artes, performing combos, and collecting Souls for attack barrages. Stored signals also strongly support party-based play: party_tactics, party_management, party_control, and party_management_loop all appear in the audit payload with relatively high confidence. Metacritic labels it Action RPG, while Steam categories add single-player and co-op support, which fits Tales-style auxiliary co-op without displacing the core single-player campaign identity. Some facets like exact topology and perspective are inferred from the remaster/store framing and franchise-typical presentation cues in the text rather than being directly spelled out, so confidence is solid but not maximal.

overall_note: The set is strong and includes the essential base game plus multiple close Tales-series neighbors that share the same party-based action-JRPG identity. Scarlet Nexus is a reasonable looser comp but sits clearly below the Tales titles because its combat and campaign structure are more action-forward and less like the classic Tales loop.
anchor_summary: Tales of Berseria Remastered is a story-led JRPG/action RPG built around a fixed party, real-time combo-driven Artes combat, character progression, overworld/town/dungeon travel, and a long-form authored campaign. Best matches should preserve the Tales-style party action-JRPG loop rather than just anime presentation or narrative tone.
gap_notes: The original Tales of Berseria is an obvious must-include base-game pairing for a remaster rail.
Other Tales entries fit better than non-series anime action RPGs because they preserve the same party combat grammar, progression structure, and campaign flow.
Scarlet Nexus is acceptable as an adjacent action-JRPG neighbor, but it is notably less similar in party control and overall structure than the Tales candidates.

title: Towerborne

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Towerborne as a side-scrolling 'Action RPG Brawler' with classic combo-based combat, four weapon styles, fast responsive encounters, and up-to-4-player co-op. The loop is hub-to-expedition-to-return: venture out from the Belfry, fight through enemies, then return to turn in quests, reforge gear, and customize builds. That supports beat_em_up as the core gameplay identity, with secondary loot_action_rpg elements from gear, crafting resources, build flexibility, and progression. The current computed taxonomy appears noisy in places, but the underlying store text strongly supports a co-op side-scrolling brawler rather than platformer or traditional fighter.

overall_note: The set captures Towerborne's core identity as a 2D co-op brawler with RPG progression reasonably well. Dragon's Crown Pro is the clearest benchmark, Castle Crashers Remastered is a strong genre neighbor, and Young Souls/River City Girls 2 are valid but somewhat looser due to mode scale or stronger emphasis on story/open exploration over the shared-session loot-brawler loop.
anchor_summary: Towerborne is a side-scrolling co-op beat-'em-up/action RPG hybrid: combo-driven lane brawling, up-to-4-player co-op, weapon/build customization, hub-to-expedition structure, and repeatable progression through gear, crafting, and difficulty scaling.
gap_notes: Dragon's Crown Pro is the strongest fit and should anchor the rail.
Castle Crashers Remastered is a strong inclusion, but its progression is lighter and more arcade-leaning than Towerborne's.
Young Souls fits the side-scrolling action-RPG brawler space well, though its co-op scale and campaign emphasis make it a bit less direct than Dragon's Crown Pro or Castle Crashers Remastered.
River City Girls 2 is relevant, but its open-city progression and classic brawler focus make it a looser match for Towerborne's loot-forward co-op expedition loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close match on side-scrolling co-op brawler combat, class/gear progression, repeated dungeon runs, and loot-driven action-RPG structure.

Strong co-op side-scrolling beat-'em-up with leveling, items, and expanded progression, but more open-ended urban brawler than loot-centric co-op action-RPG.

Castle Crashers Remastered is a strong inclusion, but its progression is lighter and more arcade-leaning than Towerborne's.
Young Souls fits the side-scrolling action-RPG brawler space well, though its co-op scale and campaign emphasis make it a bit less direct than Dragon's Crown Pro or Castle Crashers Remastered.
River City Girls 2 is relevant, but its open-city progression and classic brawler focus make it a looser match for Towerborne's loot-forward co-op expedition loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close match on side-scrolling co-op brawler combat, class/gear progression, repeated dungeon runs, and loot-driven action-RPG structure.
Excellent 4-player side-scrolling beat-'em-up comp with stage-based runs, weapon variety, and light character progression, though less loot/build focused.
Modern 2D brawler/action-RPG with gear upgrades, hub returns, and co-op, but more story/campaign driven and less tuned to 4-player repeatable party brawling.
Strong co-op side-scrolling beat-'em-up with leveling, items, and expanded progression, but more open-ended urban brawler than loot-centric co-op action-RPG.

title: Utawarerumono: ZAN 2

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a 3D hack-and-slash action RPG centered on fast-paced combat, a 20+ character roster, and 50+ story missions. The Steam detailed description gives the strongest identity signals: '3D HACK-AND-SLASH REBORN,' 'mix and match flashy combat techniques,' 'evolvable abilities,' and 'create an unstoppable team' all support a character-action core with party-management elements rather than a pure solo combo-action game. The presence of a primary story mode following Mask of Truth, a base system for interacting with companions and strengthening characters, and an online/single-player Dream Arena side mode supports a campaign-driven, mission-based structure with secondary JRPG-story-RPG traits. Evidence is strong enough to accept, but not strong enough to place it in a more specific action-RPG subtype from the allowed list.

overall_note: The rail has a strong core: the direct predecessor is essential, and the Fate/Persona/Warriors comps all map meaningfully to roster-based anime action RPG combat with story progression. The main weakness is that only the predecessor feels truly indispensable; the rest vary in how closely they match ZAN 2's specific mix of mission structure, party systems, and story-driven progression.
anchor_summary: Utawarerumono: ZAN 2 is a mission-based anime action RPG/hack-and-slash built around a large playable roster, party management, character growth, story adaptation, base interactions, and optional co-op side content.
gap_notes: Utawarerumono: ZAN is the clear must-include and best fit by a wide margin.
Fate/Extella Link is a strong gameplay neighbor thanks to roster-based flashy battlefield combat and mission structure, though its battlefield control focus is somewhat more musou-like.

Persona 5 Strikers fits well on party-based action RPG structure and story progression, but its encounter flow and dungeon/travel structure are less directly aligned than ZAN's more straightforward mission-based hack-and-slash format.

Fire Emblem Warriors: Three Hopes shares mission structure, base interactions, and squad management, but its stronger strategic layer and battlefield command emphasis make it a looser comp than the others.

Strong fit through party-based action RPG combat, character switching, progression, and story-forward structure, but its dungeon/jail flow is less like ZAN 2's cleaner mission-based format.

Meaningful overlap in large-cast action combat, base interactions, unit growth, and mission structure, but its tactical command layer and map-objective emphasis push it farther from ZAN 2's core feel.

title: Pogui

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Pogui as a side-scrolling platformer with hazardous levels, running/jumping/dashing, stamina-managed movement, and world-by-world stage progression. Metacritic explicitly calls it a 'side-scrolling precision platformer' in retro pixel art style, which strongly supports precision_platformer as the core identity. The game appears level-based across 5 themed worlds with handcrafted challenges and traversal-focused play. Enemy presence and 'defeat this nightmare' language indicate light action elements, making action_platformer a reasonable secondary archetype, but the dominant loop is precise movement and obstacle navigation rather than combat.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong because most picks target 2D challenge platformers with level-based progression and movement mastery. The best matches are the pure precision platformers; the weaker side of the set drifts toward broader action-platformers with more combat, exploration, or classic mascot-platformer structure than Pogui’s tightly hazard-driven identity.
anchor_summary: Pogui is a short single-player 2D side-scrolling precision platformer with level/world-based progression, hazard-heavy stages, dash/stamina-managed movement, quick challenge-oriented play, and light enemy interaction rather than combat-led structure.
gap_notes: The set leans a bit too much on broad action-platformers; Pogui’s strongest identity is precision platforming with short hazard stages and retry-driven movement.
Celeste and Super Meat Boy are excellent benchmarks, but the rest are progressively looser fits rather than equally tight precision comps.
Enemy presence in Pogui does not make combat-central platformers ideal matches; movement challenge should dominate comparison.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Extremely close fit: side-scrolling precision platforming, dash-and-stamina movement, hazardous handcrafted stages, and death/retry challenge structure align very strongly with Pogui.
Another essential comp for brutal 2D precision platforming: compact levels, instant retries, twitch movement mastery, and obstacle-driven play closely match Pogui’s core loop.

A very solid neighbor for difficult 2D precision platforming and hostile stage navigation, though it trends more toward sprawling platform-adventure framing than Pogui’s shorter world-based structure.
Good fit on side-scrolling, level-based world progression, enemies, and polished movement, but it reads more like a classic mascot action-platformer than a pure precision platformer.

Reasonable comparison for fluid 2D world-based platforming with enemies and mechanic variety, but its combat, co-op profile, and broader action-platformer feel make it looser than Pogui’s precision-focused identity.

title: Dice A Million

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text consistently identifies Dice A Million as a 'roguelike deckbuilder' built around randomly generated runs, combining 120+ dice for synergies, equipping passive rings, choosing among multiple hands/classes, and overcoming escalating debts and bosses. That strongly matches a run-based deck-construction game with buildcraft, metaprogression/unlocks, tactical optimization, and boss encounters. Although the theme is dice rather than literal cards, the core gameplay identity is clearly deckbuilder/card-battler adjacent, and card_battler is the closest supported archetype in the taxonomy.

overall_note: The set contains several strong dice-driven roguelike/deckbuilder comps, but also includes one clear mismatch and one looser score-chasing neighbor. The strongest fits are the actual dice-deckbuilding roguelikes.
anchor_summary: Dice A Million is a single-player run-based roguelike deckbuilder built around assembling a pool/bag of dice, layering passive ring effects, chasing heavy synergy and score scaling, and overcoming escalating debt checkpoints and bosses through tactical optimization.
gap_notes: A must-include caliber comp would be another explicit dice-pool/deckbuilding roguelike with run-based progression and synergy drafting; Astrea and SpellRogue are closest here.
Luck be a Landlord matches debt thresholds, luck-pushing, and break-the-system scoring, but its slot-machine autobattler/econ loop is more adjacent than directly similar.
Slice, Dice & Rice is a taxonomy/name mix-up and should not appear on this rail.

title: Reigns: The Witcher

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify this as a Reigns-style swipe-driven narrative/choice game: 'swipe right, swipe left,' 'thousands of storyline combinations,' 'navigate the moral maze,' puzzle-solving, unlocking deeds/characters, and leveling up Dandelion's career. That is strong evidence for a branching choice-centric design, but the allowed archetype set does not include a clear Reigns/interactive-choice/narrative-card archetype. Available action RPG or beat 'em up candidates in the current taxonomy debug payload are contradicted by the actual text, which does not support third-person melee action as the core gameplay loop. Because the gameplay identity is clear yet does not map cleanly and safely to an allowed archetype, rejection is the most accurate outcome.

overall_note: This candidate set is tightly aligned because every title is another Reigns game using the same swipe-choice, branching-run structure. The main ranking question is closeness of exact formula versus entries that add larger side systems like battles or multiplayer.
anchor_summary: Reigns: The Witcher is a Reigns-format swipe-driven narrative choice game built around binary card decisions, branching event chains, light stat/resource management, unlocks, and short-run replayability. Its gameplay identity comes from the Reigns structure first, with Witcher theming layered onto that formula rather than action RPG combat.
gap_notes: The rail correctly centers Reigns-format games rather than Witcher franchise action RPGs.
The strongest comps are the core Reigns titles and licensed Reigns variants with nearly identical decision-loop structure.
Reigns: Three Kingdoms is still relevant, but its turn-based battles and broader strategy layer make it a slightly looser fit than the earlier, purer Reigns entries.

A direct sequel that preserves the core swipe-decision structure, balance management, unlocks, and event-chain storytelling with only modest systemic additions.
Another licensed Reigns variant with the same core loop of left/right choices, reign survival, faction management, and branching ruler-specific storylines.

title: LOVE

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored description consistently identifies LOVE as a challenging retro platforming game built around 16 levels, speedrun mode, competitive scoring/leaderboards, and a unique checkpoint-respawn mechanic. That strongly supports precision_platformer over broader platformer archetypes: the core loop is stage clearing through difficult platforming challenges rather than combat, exploration, or collection. Evidence supports level-based progression, platform-navigation mechanics, score-attack replayability, mastery-heavy input demands, and no meaningful combat emphasis.

overall_note: The candidate set is very strong for a precision-platformer anchor. The top four are all credible rail inclusions, with Super Meat Boy and N++ especially aligned on level-based mastery and replay. The main distinction is that some candidates lean more exploratory or adventure-structured than LOVE’s stripped-down score-attack stage focus.
anchor_summary: LOVE is a level-based 2D precision platformer centered on difficult obstacle-course clearing, fast retries via a custom respawn/checkpoint system, and replay-oriented score/speedrun modes. Best matches should emphasize pure movement challenge, compact stage design, mastery, and minimal combat/exploration overhead.
gap_notes: No direct inclusion of other ultra-minimal level-based precision platformers built around checkpoint mastery and short stages beyond the strongest top picks.
Some candidates drift toward broader adventure/exploration platforming rather than LOVE's reductive, score/speedrun-focused structure.

Tough retry-heavy platforming is relevant, but it is more sprawling and collectible/adventure-leaning than LOVE’s reductive score-attack precision-platformer identity.

title: Supernova

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is materially contradictory. Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently present Supernova as a space-themed Suika-style matching puzzle focused on combining planets for high score, while the OpenCritic description instead describes a sci-fi MOBA/strategy game with upgradeable armies, commanders, and crafting from drops. These imply fundamentally different gameplay identities, and neither line of evidence is strong enough to cleanly map into a supported Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype with confidence. Because the bundle appears to conflate different titles or contains unreliable metadata, classification should be rejected.

overall_note: The rail has one excellent direct match, one reasonable broader puzzle neighbor, and one clearly wrong candidate caused by metadata/title mismatch.

gap_notes: Anchor metadata is conflated between a Suika-like puzzle and an unrelated sci-fi MOBA, so only candidates matching the merge-puzzle loop should score well.

title: Royal Vermin

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Royal Vermin as a 2D local platform fighter for 2-4 players, explicitly 'inspired by the best platform fighters.' The core loop is match-based PvP: players climb, throw, use ranged chargeable projectiles, knock opponents out of collapsing arenas, and aim to win rounds in fast, chaotic matches. Side-scrolling presentation, duel-focused combat, dominant combat presence, and win-match goals strongly support traditional_fighter over platformer; platforming is present but serves the fighting format rather than precision challenge progression. Party-game qualities are notable through local same-screen multiplayer and chaotic comeback-focused design, but they are secondary to the fighter identity.

overall_note: This is a very strong rail. All four candidates are genuine platform-fighter comps with close match-based PvP structure, side-view arena combat, knockout-focused rules, and overlap in local party play or competitive brawling. The top three are especially on-target benchmarks for Royal Vermin’s gameplay identity.
anchor_summary: Royal Vermin is a 2D side-view platform fighter built around fast local PvP matches for 2-4 players, ring-outs/knock-offs, hazardous evolving arenas, accessible controls, and party-friendly chaos with projectile-heavy combat.
gap_notes: The rail leans toward established platform-fighter benchmarks and does not surface a candidate specifically defined by collapsing/hazard-heavy stages and projectile-grab emphasis, which are notable Royal Vermin differentiators.
Royal Vermin is strongly local-first and casual-party oriented; some candidates skew more toward competitive online ecosystems than same-screen party chaos, though they still fit well.

candidate_reviews.rationale: The clearest genre benchmark: side-view platform-fighter matches built on launch/ring-out rules, stage hazards, items, multiplayer free-for-alls, and broad party accessibility.
